revision.text:
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin-kan]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin-kan. Other Chokunin-kan were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin-kan, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin-kan and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin-kan and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin-kan occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin-kan, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin-kan === {{Main|Chokunin-kan}} '''Chokunin-kan''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

It retained the distinction between Chokunin-kan and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.

Chokunin-kan occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin-kan, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin-kan === {{Main|Chokunin-kan}} '''Chokunin-kan''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin-kan]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin-kan. Other Chokunin-kan were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin-kan, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin-kan and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin-kan and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin-kan occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin-kan, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin-kan === {{Main|Chokunin-kan}} '''Chokunin-kan''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

[[Category:Japanese civil service]] [[Category:Government of the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kōtōkan]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:高等官]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ōtōkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:qq]]

{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chokunin|ja|勅任官}} * {{ill|Sōnin-kan|ja|奏任官}} * {{ill|Hannin-kan|ja|判任官}}
== External Links == * 「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」{{Wikiref|JACAR-A03020001000|JACAR：A03020001000}} * 「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第九十六号・高等官官等俸給令」{{Wikiref|JACAR-A03020130600|JACAR：A03020130600}}

Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

revision.text:
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq1088500|JACAR：A15qq1088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February qq, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15qq1667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq224qq00|JACAR：A15qq224qq00}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020qq4700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15qq2439800|JACAR：A15qq2439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

[[Category:11]][[en:Kōtōkan]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōtōkan]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kōtōkan}}[[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōtōkan}}

{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 2]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.

'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[officials]] established in [[1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[civil service|civil]] and [[military ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan rank, appointed directly by the [[Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the rank below Kōtōkan.
