It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

<!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11672687]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[:en:1890|1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[:en:1946|1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

<!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11672687}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672687]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[:en:1890|1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[:en:1946|1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[:en:1890|1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[:en:1946|1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.

Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.

=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

<!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11672687}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672687]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[:en:1890|1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[:en:1946|1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

<!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--><!--Kōtōkan--> [[Category:Abolished Administrative Positions in Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11672687}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672687]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[:en:1890|1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[:en:1946|1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from [[:en:1890|1890]] (Meiji 23) until its abolition in [[:en:1946|1946]] (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the [[:en:Prime Minister of Japan|Prime Minister]] before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into [[:en:Civil service|civil]] and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]].
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chokunin|ja|勅任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokunin|1=chokuninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399919}} * {{ill|Sōnin-kan|ja|奏任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōnin-kan|1=sōninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445526}} * {{ill|Hannin-kan|ja|判任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hannin-kan|1=hanninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396615}}

== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|WD=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister}} before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution}}, national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into {{ill|Civil service|en|Civil service|WD=Q11771944|lt=civil}} and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}.
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chokunin|ja|勅任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokunin|1=chokuninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399919}} * {{ill|Sōnin-kan|ja|奏任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōnin-kan|1=sōninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445526}} * {{ill|Hannin-kan|ja|判任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hannin-kan|1=hanninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396615}}
== External Links == * 「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」{{Wikiref|JACAR-A03020001000|JACAR：A03020001000}} * 「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第九十六号・高等官官等俸給令」{{Wikiref|JACAR-A03020130600|JACAR：A03020130600}}

{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in {{ill|1886|en|1886|WD=Q7822|lt=1886}} (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890}} (Meiji 23) until its abolition in {{ill|1946|en|1946|WD=Q18610|lt=1946}} (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|WD=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister}} before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution}}, national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into {{ill|Civil service|en|Civil service|WD=Q11771944|lt=civil}} and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}.
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}}, national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into {{ill|Civil service|en|Civil service|WD=Q11771944|lt=civil|1=Civil service}} and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]]
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in {{ill|1886|en|1886|WD=Q7822|lt=1886|1=1886}} (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from {{ill|1890|en|1890|WD=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}} (Meiji 23) until its abolition in {{ill|1946|en|1946|WD=Q18610|lt=1946|1=1946}} (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|WD=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}}, national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into {{ill|Civil service|en|Civil service|WD=Q11771944|lt=civil|1=Civil service}} and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|WD=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}}, national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into {{ill|Civil service|en|Civil service|WD=Q11771944|lt=civil|1=Civil service}} and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高等官]]
{{Citations needed|date=August 2018}}
'''Kōtōkan''' (高等官) was a classification of [[:en:Officials|Officials]] established in {{ill|1886|en|1886|qid=Q7822|lt=1886|1=1886}} (Meiji 19) and used under the [[:en:Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Meiji Constitution]] from {{ill|1890|en|1890|qid=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}} (Meiji 23) until its abolition in {{ill|1946|en|1946|qid=Q18610|lt=1946|1=1946}} (Shōwa 21)<ref>「御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020001000、御署名原本・明治十九年・勅令第六号・高等官官等俸給令（国立公文書館）</ref><ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A04017814600|JACAR：A04017814600}}（Pages 2–3）</ref>. It Ranked above the [[Hannin-kan]].
== Development of Kōtōkan == === March 1886: Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance === After transitioning from the [[:en:Dajōkan|Dajōkan]] system to a cabinet system with the [[Imperial Ordinance on the Powers of the Cabinet]] on December 22, 1885, the ordinance of February 26, 1886 (Meiji 19, Imperial Ordinance No. 2) standardized administrative roles. Ministers supervised their respective officials, and appointments or dismissals of [[Sōnin-kan]] and above required approval by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} before being presented to the Emperor for approval<ref name="February 1886 Cabinet Ordinance">「各省ノ官制通則ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111077600、公文類聚・第十編・明治十九年・第二巻・官職一・官職総（国立公文書館）</ref>.
The establishment of the '''Kōtōkan''' classification followed on March 12, 1886, with the [[Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 6). The officials were divided into [[Chokunin]] and [[Sōnin-kan]], with the [[Imperial Order of Appointment]] (Shinnin-shiki) introduced for some Chokunin. Other Chokunin were subdivided into two Ranks, and Sōnin-kan into six Ranks<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 3–4）</ref>. Kōtōkan salaries were provided as annual payments<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15111088500|JACAR：A15111088500}}（Pages 5, 7）</ref>.
Under the Dajōkan system, there was no distinction between Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan in Rank structures. However, this ordinance reformed the system, separating the Kōtōkan and Hannin-kan<ref>「官制等級改定」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A24010436500、太政類典・第二編・明治四年～明治十年（国立公文書館）</ref>.
=== July 1891: Appointment and Salaries Ordinance === After the [[Promulgation of the Meiji Constitution]] on February 11, 1889, the December 24, 1889 [[Cabinet Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 135) stipulated that appointments and dismissals of Chokunin and local government heads required Cabinet approval<ref>「内閣官制ヲ定ム」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A15111667400、公文類聚・第十三編・明治二十二年・第二巻（国立公文書館）</ref>.
Subsequently, on July 24, 1891, the [[Appointment and Salaries Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 82) abolished the earlier Kōtōkan Ranks and Salaries Ordinance. It retained the distinction between Chokunin and Sōnin-kan, but abolished official Ranks<ref name="July 1891 Appointment Ordinance">{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112241100|JACAR：A15112241100}}</ref>.
=== November 1891: Classification Table === On November 14, 1891, the [[High-Ranking Civil and Military Officials Classification Table]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) established a 10-Rank system. Chokunin occupied the first to third Ranks, while Sōnin-kan occupied the fourth to tenth Ranks<ref>「御署名原本・明治二十四年・勅令第二百十五号・文武高等官官職等級表」JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター）Ref.A03020114700</ref>.
=== November 1892: Restoration of Rank and Salary System === In November 1892, the [[Rank and Salary Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 96) reinstated the Rank system, replacing the 1891 ordinances<ref>{{Wikiref|JACAR-A15112439800|JACAR：A15112439800}}</ref>.
=== April 1946: Abolition of Kōtōkan === Under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]], the Kōtōkan system was abolished on April 1, 1946, with the [[Officials Appointment and Classification Ordinance]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 190). Kōtōkan Ranks were reclassified into first and second-grade officials<ref name="Abolition of Kōtōkan 1946"/>.
== Officials under the Meiji Constitution == [[File:Kanri01.png|thumb|300px|Kōtōkan included parenthetical officials: Chokunin, Sōnin-kan, and Hannin-kan.]] Under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}}, national employees were classified as [[Kōtōkan]], [[Hannin-kan]], and others (employees, contractors, etc.), with distinct legal protections and privileges.
Kōtōkan were divided into {{ill|Civil service|en|Civil service|qid=Q11771944|lt=civil|1=Civil service}} and [[:en:military Ranks|military Ranks]].
=== Chokunin === {{Main|Chokunin}} '''Chokunin''' was the highest Kōtōkan Rank, appointed directly by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.
=== Sōnin-kan === {{Main|Sōnin-kan}} '''Sōnin-kan''' comprised Ranks three to nine, appointed by the Emperor based on recommendations by the Prime Minister or relevant ministers<ref name="March 1886 Kōtōkan Ordinance"/>.
=== Hannin-kan === {{Main|Hannin-kan}} '''Hannin-kan''' was the Rank below Kōtōkan.
